inputs.description: Standard use case with clear source material
inputs.prompt: Source material: 'The biggest mistake people make when learning to cook is following recipes too literally. Professional chefs don't measure everything. They taste constantly. They adjust. The difference between a home cook and a professional isn't technique — it's the willingness to deviate from the plan. A recipe is a suggestion, not a contract. Once you understand the principle behind a technique, you can adapt it to whatever you have in the fridge. That's when cooking stops being stressful and starts being creative.' Core point: Stop following recipes literally — learn the principle, then improvise. Target length: 55 seconds.

Source material: 'Satellite imagery has completely transformed deforestation monitoring. In the past, illegal logging in remote areas could go undetected for months. Now, systems like Global Forest Watch use Landsat and Sentinel-2 data to detect tree cover loss within days. The resolution has improved dramatically — we can now detect clearings as small as 30 meters across. But the technology creates a paradox: we can see the destruction in near real-time, but enforcement capacity in most tropical countries has not kept pace. We know exactly where the forests are being cut. We just can't stop it fast enough.' Core point: We can see deforestation in real-time now, but seeing it and stopping it are different problems. Target length: 50 seconds.

Source material: 'Most people drink coffee wrong. They drink it first thing in the morning when cortisol is already high. The best time for coffee is 90 minutes after waking.' Core point: Delay your first coffee by 90 minutes. Target length: 30 seconds.

Source material: 'Denmark's approach to elderly care is fundamentally different from most Western countries. Instead of building larger nursing homes, they have been systematically reducing institutional care and investing in home-based support. The concept is called hverdagsrehabilitering — everyday rehabilitation. The idea is simple: instead of doing things for elderly people, you help them regain the ability to do things for themselves. A 2019 study showed that municipalities using this model reduced permanent nursing home placements by 22% over five years. The elderly reported higher life satisfaction. The cost per person went down.' Core point: Denmark's approach to elderly care focuses on helping people regain independence, not managing decline. Target length: 55 seconds. Drive to full video: Yes.

Source material: 'There are some interesting things about how habits work. People often struggle with habits. It takes time. Consistency is important. You should start small.' Core point: Something about habits. Target length: 45 seconds.

Source material: 'The suicide rate among veterinarians is three times higher than the general population. They deal with euthanasia decisions daily. Many enter the profession because they love animals, only to discover that a significant part of the job involves ending lives. Financial stress compounds the problem — the average vet school debt in the US exceeds $180,000, while starting salaries are often under $90,000. Professional culture discourages vulnerability. Colleagues who seek help are sometimes seen as not tough enough for the job.' Core point: Veterinarians face a silent mental health crisis driven by euthanasia decisions, debt, and professional stigma. Target length: 55 seconds. Note: Handle with care — no shock hooks, no trivialising.

inputs.description: Source material in bullet points, not prose
inputs.prompt:
Source material:
- Average person checks phone 96 times per day (Asurion 2023)
- Each check averages 3 minutes 15 seconds
- Total: ~5.2 hours daily on phone
- 73% of checks are habitual, not intentional
- Greyscale mode reduces phone usage by ~37% (Duke University)
- Most effective intervention: charging phone in a different room overnight
Core point: You check your phone 96 times a day and most of it is unconscious — but greyscale mode cuts usage by a third. Target length: 40 seconds.

Source material: 'The favelas of Rio de Janeiro have produced some of the world's most innovative urban solutions. Residents built their own water systems, electricity networks, and waste collection cooperatives decades before the government acknowledged these communities existed. The baile funk scene generates more economic activity per square metre than most formal entertainment districts. Now, tech startups are emerging from favelas — Cufa, a community organisation, trained 15,000 favela residents in digital skills during the pandemic. The favela is not a problem to be solved. It is a laboratory of resilience.' Core point: Favelas are innovation hubs, not just poverty zones. Target length: 50 seconds.

inputs.description: Very long source needing aggressive compression
inputs.prompt: Source material: [2,500-word transcript from a 20-minute interview about the history of containerised shipping, covering Malcolm McLean's invention of the standardised container in 1956, the resistance from dock workers' unions, the transformation of port cities, the environmental cost of global shipping, the Ever Given Suez Canal blockage as a wake-up call, and current efforts to develop zero-emission container ships using ammonia fuel cells. The interviewee is a maritime historian.] Core point: One metal box changed the world economy — and now the industry it created has to reinvent itself to survive. Target length: 55 seconds. Drive to full video: Yes.
